source_topic: How a GPU actually works — and why it crushes a CPU at graphics and AI

scenes.narration: Your CPU has 8 cores. Your GPU has sixteen thousand, three hundred and eighty-four. That's not a typo.

props.title: GPU POWER
props.subtitle: 16,384 cores vs 8. Here's why that matters.

scenes.narration: It's not the count. CPU cores are complex and versatile. GPU cores are simple, identical, moving in lockstep.

props.fallbackText: CPU has 8 complex cores. GPU has 16,384 simple identical ones.

scenes.narration: CPUs handle anything — branching, logic, decisions. GPUs run one instruction across thousands of cores simultaneously. That architecture is called SIMD.
Neural networks are just chains of matrix multiplication. Billions of independent operations per layer, each requiring nothing from the others.
GPUs run matrix math ninety-three times faster than CPUs. Not smarter — just built for exactly this kind of work.

props.label: faster than CPU on matrix multiplication

scenes.narration: ChatGPT, Stable Diffusion, Sora — all trained on GPUs running billions of matrix multiplications in parallel, every forward pass.
NVIDIA just cut AI training cost by four times. The GPU isn't a chip anymore — it's the engine of intelligence.

props.text: NVIDIA just cut AI training cost by 4×. The GPU isn't a graphics card anymore.

scenes.narration: One GPU breakthrough made the entire AI revolution possible. Follow for one concept a day.

caption:
Your GPU doesn't just run games. It's why every AI model exists — and there's a simple architectural reason why.

GPUs run one instruction across 16,384 cores at once (SIMD). Neural networks are pure matrix math — perfectly parallel by design.

Result: 93× faster than a CPU. Every AI model you've used was trained on thousands of these. 💡

NVIDIA's Vera Rubin just made it 4× cheaper to train them.

Save this for the next time someone asks why AI needs GPUs.

[GPU, AI, ComputerScience, DeepLearning, NVIDIA]
